en: Well, shave me down and call me a mole rat. You found another mammoth!
ko: 이봐, 나 밀어버리고 두더지쥐라고 불러. 또 다른 매머드를 찾았네.

en: Where? Wait a minute, I thought mammoths were extinct.
What are you looking at me for? I don't know. Maybe because you're a mammoth?
ko: 왜 나를 보는 거야? 모르겠는데. 네가 매머드니까? 나? 말도

en: Me? Don't be ridiculous. I'm not a mammoth. I'm a possum!
ko: 나? 말도 안 돼. 난 매머드가 아니야. 난 주머니쥐야. 주머니쥐는

en: This is a mole rat. You can see it's mostly hairless and pretty ugly,
ko: 안 돼. 난 매머드가 아니야. 난 주머니쥐야. 이건 두더지쥐야. 보다시피 거의 털이 없고

en: kind of like what Sid would look like if he shaved all his fur off.
ko: 꽤 못생겼지. Sid가 털을 다 밀면 이렇게 생겼을 거야.

en: Me? Don't be ridiculous. I'm not a mammoth. I'm a possum!
ko: 나? 말도 안 돼. 난 매머드가 아니야. 난 주머니쥐야. 주머니쥐는

en: This is a possum. They're known for climbing trees, coming out at night,
and playing dead when threatened. And by the way, Ellie's reaction here is another
great line we can use for practicing connected speech. Check it out.
ko: Ellie의 반응도 연결 발음 연습에 좋은 대사야. 봐봐.

en: What are you looking at me for?
ko: 왜 나를 보는 거야? 모르겠는데. 네가 매머드니까? 나? 말도

en: Basically, natives oftentimes just say, what are you? And where are you? What are you doing?
ko: Why are you looking at me for? 원어민들은 주로 what are you, where are you라고 말해. What

en: Where are you going? What are you looking at me for?
ko: are you doing? Where are you going? What are you looking at me for?
